At the same time, some scenarios, like Joule agents making operational decisions, cannot tolerate high latency or missing data. Data tiering is how you manage this trade‑off between cost and performance without compromising AI quality and trust.

1.2 From Reporting to Autonomous Decisions

Historically, data tiering conversations in SAP environments focused on:

  • Reducing database size (especially in ERP and BW)
  • Managing performance for reporting and planning
  • Meeting retention and compliance requirements at lower cost

In the Autonomous Enterprise, the stakes are higher. Tiering decisions now influence:

  • Real-time operational analytics: e.g., S/4HANA operational KPIs, supply chain control towers, same‑day profitability
  • Tactical management reporting: month‑end/quarter‑end analysis, scenario planning, rolling forecasts
  • Strategic and historical analysis: multi‑year trends, structural cost analysis, sustainability reporting
  • AI and agentic use cases: Joule agents executing workflows, AI copilots assisting users in SAP Fiori and line-of-business apps,RAG‑style (retrieval‑augmented generation) scenarios pulling context into LLMs, ML models trained on long‑range historical, IoT, or behavioral data

Each of these has a different tolerance for latency, freshness, data volume, and cost. A well‑designed tiering strategy ensures that:

  • Hot data is available at in‑memory speed for agents and operational analytics
  • Warm data supports mid‑term analysis and planning without over‑provisioning HANA
  • Cold data and archives are kept cost‑efficiently but still discoverable, governable, and usable for ML and compliance

2. Data Tiering Concepts & Terminology:

Before going into SAP Datasphere specifics, it helps to establish a clear, neutral vocabulary for data tiering that you can reuse with architects, business stakeholders, and AI teams.

2.1 What Is Data Tiering?  Data tiering is a data management strategy where you separate data into different temperature tiers and storage technologies and core dimensions classifying it:

  1. Access frequency: How often is the data read? Hourly, daily, monthly, rarely?
  2. Update requirement: Does the data need to be updated after initial load? Is it transactional (insert/update/delete) or more append‑only?
  3. Performance & latency:  How fast do queries need to return? Does an AI agent or operational dashboard depend on sub‑second responses?
  4. Data Freshness:  Does the data need to be real‑time, near real‑time, or is daily/weekly refresh enough?
  5. Retention period: How many years must the data be kept for legal, audit, or business reasons?
  6. Data volume: How large is the dataset today, and how fast is it growing?
  7. Security & compliance: Are there specific regulations (GDPR, industry rules) that affect where data can be stored and who can access it?
  8. Business criticality (MOST IMPORTANT): If this data is slow or unavailable, what is the impact on the business and on AI‑driven processes?

In SAP terms, data tiering is part of multi‑temperature data management: you keep the most valuable, time‑critical data on your fastest (and most expensive) infrastructure, while moving less critical data to cheaper, slower layers without losing access to it.

2.2 The Classical 3 Tiers: Hot, Warm, Cold Most SAP data tiering discussions (HANA , BW/4HANA, Datasphere) use the same three core concepts, with slightly different technical realizations. Remote Data Tier is an data access pattern.

2.3 Data Tiering vs. Related Concepts in SAP: It is useful to separate data tiering from other, sometimes overlapping, concepts:

  • Data aging (e..g SAP S/4HANA): Framework that moves older data from hot in‑memory storage to warm disk‑based storage within the same HANA system. It reduces memory footprint while keeping data accessible. This is a mechanism that supports a tiering strategy at the application level.
  • Data Tiering Optimization (DTO) in BW/4HANA: Classifies data in an ADSO as hot, warm, or cold, and maps each temperature to a specific storage technology (e.g., HANA in‑memory, HANA NSE/extension nodes, SAP IQ). DTO is a tool that applies tiering policies in BW/4HANA.
  • Embedded Data Lake / data lake integration: Storing large volumes of structured/semi‑structured/unstructured data on low‑cost cloud object storage (e.g., embedded data lake in SAP Datasphere, or external data lakes). This typically supports warm/cold tiers for large volumes.
  • Virtualization / federation: Data remains in the source system (S/4HANA, Ariba, ECC, BW, non‑SAP DB, data lake) and is accessed on demand via remote tables or views. Virtualization is not a tier by itself, but an access pattern that can implement hot, warm, or cold tiers without physical replication.

2.4 Analytical & AI Consumption Patterns:  Tiering decisions should start from the intended consumption patterns, not from the storage technology. Key patterns include:

  • Real‑time operational analytics:  Examples: same‑day order‑to‑cash KPIs, supply chain control tower, production monitoring.Requirements: hot data, very low latency, typically in‑memory HANA, minimal joins across slow systems.
  • Tactical reporting & planning: Examples: monthly financial close, rolling forecasts, quarterly performance reviews. Requirements: mix of hot and warm data, acceptable latency in seconds; may combine data from replicated and virtual sources.
  • Strategic, historical, and regulatory analysis: Examples: multi‑year profitability, sustainability KPIs, root‑cause analysis over several years. Requirements: heavy use of warm and cold tiers; performance is important but not sub‑second; cost and retention dominate.
  • AI & ML training: Examples: training predictive models, building recommendation systems, anomaly detection, LLM fine‑tuning. Requirements: large volumes of warm/cold data; batch access; cost‑efficient storage more important than interactive speed.
  • AI & agent inference / context retrieval (RAG):  Examples: Joule or custom agents answering questions or taking actions using business data context. Requirements: hot data products with low latency and rich semantics; limited but well‑curated warm data for extended context; some cold data may be pre‑indexed or summarized.

Each pattern maps to a different  temperature mix which later translates into concrete options in SAP Datasphere (local tables (In-memory/Disk), remote tables, object storage (Delta-tables), etc.).

2.5 Temperature as a Policy, Not Just a Location: A subtle but important point: data temperature is a policy, not just a physical storage location.For example:

  1. A table partition might be “hot” for the current year and “warm” for previous years, even though the table itself still lives in SAP Datasphere.
  2. An object in an embedded data lake (Object store) might be “warm” when actively used for ML training, then become “cold” once the model is trained and only needs occasional retraining.
  3. Data can move hot → warm → cold as it ages (time‑based policies) or cold → warm → hot when certain events or AI scenarios increase its business relevance (usage‑based policies).

This policy‑driven view becomes especially important in SAP Datasphere and Business Data Cloud, where you design data products and spaces that must support changing business and AI requirements over time.

2.6 Data tiering characteristics: 

Data TierPerformanceCostData VolumeData UpdatesTypical Business Scenarios
HOTBest / Ultra-FastHighLow-MediumHighOperational reporting, AI Agents, Joule context, SAC dashboards, Open Orders, Current Inventory, Planning, Master Data
WARMMediumMediumMedium-HighHigh-MediumHistorical operational analytics (1–5 years), Supply Chain analytics, Procurement spend analysis, Customer analytics, Finance trending, AI/ML
COLDLowLowMassiveLow/NoneAudit, Compliance, Archived ERP/BW history, Long-term retention, Historical IoT data, Delta lake tables, AI/ML
REMOTEVariable (Source Dependent)MinimalLowVery HighVirtualized access to S/4HANA, BW/4HANA, SAP HANA, Snowflake, Databricks, Azure Fabric, data discovery and low-frequency access scenarios

 

3. SAP Datasphere Architecture and Strategy for Data Tiering: 

Data tiering in SAP Datasphere is built on top of SAP HANA Cloud and the Business Data Cloud architecture. At a high level, you combine local tables, spaces, storage options, and access patterns to implement hot, warm, and cold tiers without losing business context. Remote table will point to directly to source without copying for the remote tier data.

3.1 Key Building Blocks

  • Tenant Sizing/Configuration: Based on the requirements select the storage and memory.
  • Datasphere Spaces: Logical workspaces that isolate data, users, and data storage resources (memory, disk, file storage).You can dedicate spaces to domains (Finance, Supply Chain), regions, or workloads (AI lab vs. standard reporting).
  • Data Builder (Data engineering & Modeling): Remote/local tables, views, data flows, replication flows, transformation flow, Analytic model etc. This is where you design physical/virtual placement and thus tiering.
  • Connections & Data Federation Layer: Connects to SAP S/4HANA, BW/4HANA, ECC, SuccessFactors, Ariba, non‑SAP DBs, data lakes, and SaaS APIs. Foundation for virtual access (remote tables) and data replication the two main tiering levers.

3.2 Storage & Access Options Relevant for Tiering:  Under the hood, Datasphere uses SAP HANA Cloud as a database.

  • Local Tables: Physically replicate and store the data.
  • Remote Tables / Virtualization: : No replication; queries are pushed down to the source (e.g., S/4HANA, BW, external DB, data lake)
  • View Persistency: Persisted views/materializations in Datasphere to speed up complex logic. Often used as a performance buffer between raw inbound data and reporting, instead of keeping everything hot.

3.4. Data Tiering Options in SAP Datasphere

Remote Tier: Remote Tables that point to data in source systems and runs queries via federation without data copy.

Hot Tier: Local Tables in In-memory storage. Local tables physically stored in the SAP HANA Cloud database of your Datasphere tenant (column store).

Warm Tier: Local Tables in Disk storage. Local tables physically stored in the SAP HANA Native Storage Extension (NSE) is a built‑in warm data option in SAP HANA Cloud to keep data on disk instead of fully in memory. It combines disk‑based storage with the in‑memory to lower costs while keeping acceptable performance.

Cold Tier: Local Tables in File storage. Local tables(delta tables) physically stored in the object store (HANA Data Lake Files) of your Datasphere tenant for structured, semistructured, and unstructured data sets.

4. SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

5. Design Patterns & Typical Scenarios

5.1 Pattern 1 – Operational & Agentic Core with Remote, Hot, Warm, and Cold Tiers.

Intent: Support real-time operational decisions and AI agents with the latest business context, keep monthly and quarterly analytics on warm transactional history, and move multi-year data to cold lake storage for audit, compliance, and ML training.

Common scenarios:

  • S/4HANA‑centric processes: Finance, Order‑to‑Cash, Procure‑to‑Pay, inventory, production.
  • A relatively small set of promoted tables: Daily Revenue, Open Orders, Customer 360 Core, etc.
  • Monthly, quarterly, and yearly reporting on transactional history.
  • Operational dashboards, control towers, exception monitoring.
  • Joule/agent scenarios that need real‑time, deterministic context.
  • Audit, regulatory, and historical intelligence use cases.

Architecture view:

Remote tier (Remote tables):

  • Real-time operational data is accessed directly from source applications.This tier is used when the business needs the latest committed state without waiting for replication.
  • Best suited for: Critical real-operational reports, Control-tower dashboards, Time-sensitive AI agents

Warm + Hot, Data warehouse & database ( Local tables-Disk/In-memory )

  • Warm (default): most replicated tables from S/4 and other apps land on HANA disk/NSE. Older partitions of the same transactional tables (12–36 months), used for monthly/quarterly reporting and trend analysis.
  • Hot (explicit): only the most critical slices (e.g., current year postings, open documents, key master data, control tables) are switched to in‑memory for ultra‑fast queries.

Cold tier, Data Lake storage (Local tables-Files)

  • Acts as the foundation of the data fabric and Lake storage holds multi-year historical facts, logs, IoT data, archived detail, and deep history that is rarely accessed but required for audit or ML training.
  • Best suited for: Model/ML training and retraining, Rare deep-dive analytics and Audit and compliance

Design guidance

  • Keep schema, semantics, and business definitions consistent across remote, hot, warm, and cold so that data products can span all tiers seamlessly.
  • Use partitioning on the warm tier so that the active 12–36 month range behaves like “hot” for most analytical queries.
  • Separate your models by purpose:
    • Operational / real-time models should combine remote + hot + current warm.
    • Union models: Remote+Hot+Warm+Cold.
    • Historical / audit / ML models should extend into the cold tier only when needed.
  • Promote only the smallest business-critical slice to hot; keep the broader history warm and the full archive cold.

Example: 1. SAP Datasphere Native Connection with SAP S/4HANA: Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering 

Hot Tier: Current year data (2026)
Warm Tier: Previous two completed years (2024,2025)
Cold Tier: All data older (2023 and earlier)

1. UNIONing Sales Order Hot+Warm+Cold Tier Data, Fact Graphical View

2. Data Analysis View Showing - Analytic Model, Fact Model, Multi-Tier Tables and Replication Flow objects. 

3. Testing

Example: 2. SAP BDC Formation with SAP S/4HANA: Sales Order Data Product with below persisted data tiering 

5.2 Pattern 2 – Warm‑First Datasphere Core, Selectively Promoted → Hot/Cold

(Default warm in Datasphere, tuned over time up or down)

Intent: Start by loading everything needed for analytics into the warm tier of SAP Datasphere (default disk storage), then rebalance over time: move critical subsets up to hot and aging/low‑value data down to cold.

Common scenarios

  • Typical SAP Datasphere rollout where data replication lands in warm (Disk/HANA NSE) by default.
  • Traditional BI / reporting, planning, and first wave of AI use cases.
  • Customers that want simplicity first, then optimization.

Architecture view

Warm tier as default (Datasphere core Disk Storage)

  • All relevant operational and external data needed for reporting and AI is replicated/ingested into warm local tables.
  • Characteristics:
    • Good general‑purpose performance
    • Simple to model (everything is in one analytical environment)
    • Most dashboards, planning models, and initial agent use cases

Hot tier (promoted, latency‑critical slices)

  • After observing usage and SLAs, promote key tables and partitions to in‑memory hot for:
    • Mission‑critical dashboards and High‑frequency queries
    • AI agents needing sub‑second context
  • Typical candidates:
    • Current‑period transactional slices (e.g., last 30–90 days)
    • High‑value master data (customers, products, locations)
    • Control / security / data access lookup tables

Cold tier (demoted aging or low‑value data)

  • As data ages or is rarely accessed, move older partitions from warm into lake storage (HDFS/object store).
  • Characteristics:
    • Read‑only or minimal updates.
    • Year‑over‑year, long‑term trend analysis, audit and regulatory requirements
    • Occasional ML retraining on full history
  • The warehouse/lakehouse keeps a logical view that transparently spans warm + cold.

Remote tier (where appropriate): For some source systems, keep data remote and blend it with warm/hot only where necessary.

Design guidance

  • Start by designing semantic models and data products against the warm tier; this keeps onboarding simple.
  • Define lifecycle rules:
    • Age‑based: e.g., after 12-24 months in warm, move to cold.
    • Criticality‑based: e.g., if a dataset feeds a critical report/agent, promote its latest partitions to hot.
  • For AI:
    • Keep agent‑critical context (current state, key masters, control rules) in hot or the hottest partitions of warm.
    • Use warm for general analytics, trend analysis, and feature engineering.
    • Store long‑term histories in cold but link them semantically so agents and analysts can still reach them when necessary (e.g., via separate historical data products).

Example:  Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering: 

5.3 Pattern 3 – Cold‑First Datasphere Object Store, Selectively Promoted → Warm/Hot

(Everything lands in the lake, then is “pulled up” by importance)

Intent: Default to landing all data in the cold tier(cheap, scalable lake storage), then promote only critical data into warm and hot tiers based on actual access patterns and performance needs.

Common scenarios

  • High volume, low-moderate data updates, Mostly data batch processing (ingest → transform → aggregate).
  • Hourly, every 4-hrs, daily or even weekly once batch jobs.
  • Cloud‑scale data (IoT, clickstream, logs, partner feeds) combined with SAP business data.
  • Organizations with strong cost pressure and many unknown future use cases.
  • Heavy AI / ML experimentation and data science workloads.

Architecture view

Cold tier as landing zone (Local tables-Files)

  • All raw and detailed data (transactions, events, logs, IoT, external feeds) lands first in the HDLF/cold lake.
  • Acts as, long‑term system of record and single source for all higher tiers.
  • Delete Previous Versions: Local tables store high volume of data, including historical data. When data is updated, a new version is created and stored. Keeping all these versions consume a lot of memory, and can affect performance. You must clean them up once they have been consumed by flows to optimize storage costs and improve performance.

Warm tier (curated subsets in the lakehouse / warehouse)

  • Frequently used, cleansed, and modelled subsets are loaded from the lake into warm local tables in SAP Datasphere.
  • Characteristics:
    • Structured, governed, optimized for analytical workloads
    • Drives standard BI, tactical reporting, and many AI feature sets
  • Only a fraction of lake data is promoted here at any given time.

Hot tier (very selective, performance‑critical slices)

  • Small number of tables/partitions further promoted from warm to in‑memory hot for:
    • Executive dashboards
    • Time‑critical planning and simulations
    • High‑throughput AI inference and agents
  • Typically:
    • Recent periods (e.g., current month/quarter)
    • Narrow sets of attributes and key metrics

Remote tier (optional)

  • Some data may remain in source systems and be accessed virtually instead of being landed in the lake (e.g., SAP SaaS applications).
  • Treated logically as warm/cold depending on latency and SLA.

Design guidance

  • Treat the cold lake as default; movement to warm/hot must be justified by:
    • Access frequency
    • Latency requirements
    • Business criticality
  • Automate usage‑driven promotion:
    • Monitor query logs on lake and warm tables.
    • Identify top N datasets to copy or materialize in warm.
    • From warm, promote an even smaller top of top to hot.
  • For AI:
    • Training and experimentation pull broadly from the cold lake.
    • Production agents and data products should rely primarily on warm/hot, with controlled fallbacks to cold for rare, deep questions.

Example:  Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering:

6. Best practices and Recommendations for Data Tiering:  

Remote tier:

  • Use remote tables when you need real‑time S/4HANA, LoB Apps, or source data with no replication delay.
  • Keep remote queries narrow and push filters and aggregates down to the source. Always filter by time, company code, plant, etc. and Push filters and aggregates down to the source to avoid large data transfers.
  • Avoid overusing remote access for heavy self‑service; replicate to warm if queries become frequent or complex.
  • Use remote + local table together: Keep always‑hot context (small master data, control tables) in local hot and Join remote operational facts with local hot masters for fast, enriched views.
  • If a remote dataset becomes high‑volume or heavily used:  Replicate it into warm and optionally promote the most critical slice to hot.

Hot tier, In‑memory (ultra‑fast, mission‑critical):

When to use ?

  • Data powers mission‑critical dashboards, planning, or AI agents.
  • You need sub‑second to low‑second response and high concurrency.
  • Data changes frequently and is used continuously (daily / hourly).

What to put in hot ?

  • Current period slices of key transactional tables (e.g., last 30–90 days).
  • High‑value master data (Customer, Material, Plant, BP, cost center, etc.).
  • Control and data access control tables used in joins and authorization logic.
  • Persisted outputs of core semantic models and AI‑critical data products (e.g., Daily Revenue, Open Orders, Customer 360 Core).

Best practices:

  • Keep hot small and focused: Frequently accessed, Feeding critical reports or Joule/AI agents, needed for very recent periods.
  • Partition by time and business keys: Make only the most recent partitions hot; keep older ones warm/cold.
  • Materialize complex logic: Persist heavy joins and KPI logic into hot tables (via View persistency/Transformation Flows) instead of recomputing in every query.
  • Minimize width: Only store required columns; move verbose or rarely used attributes to warm/cold dimensions.
  • Protect hot capacity: Use separate spaces or roles to keep ad‑hoc/lab workloads off production hot tables.

Warm tier (HANA disk / NSE):

When to use? 

  • Default tier for replicated SAP and non‑SAP data in Datasphere.
  • You need good performance for 12–36 months of history, but not in‑memory speed.
  • Data is still updatable and frequently used for reporting and planning.

What to put in warm?

  • 1-2-3 years of transactional fact tables (FI, SD, MM, PP, CO, etc.) and less time‑critical master data and lookup tables.
  • Curated, harmonised reporting models that are widely reused.
  • Staging/curated tables used for feature engineering and AI training.

Best practices:

  • Treat warm as your default landing zone for replication.
  • SAP‑managed and customer‑managed data products can be replicated into local table Ideal for Monthly/quarterly reporting, Cross‑domain analytics or Standard AI feature sets and model training.
  • Use time‑based partitioning so:
    • Recent partitions (e.g., last 12–18 months) behave near‑hot.
    • Older partitions are candidates to move to cold.
  • Apply HANA Cloud warm‑store sizing rules and do not let warm grow uncontrolled.
  • Use warm as a “parking lot”:
    • Move data from hot → warm as it ages.
    • Keep still occasionally needed data here before sending it to cold.
  • Monitor usage:
    • Promote warm tables/partitions to hot if they become latency‑critical.
    • Demote rarely used warm data to cold to free capacity.

Cold tier (HDLF / object store):

When to use? 

  • Data is rarely accessed, mostly for audit, compliance, or deep historical analytics.
  • You need very low storage cost and can tolerate high latency.
  • Ideal for long‑term retention and cloud‑scale AI/ML training.

What to put in cold?

  • Detailed line‑item history older than 3–5+ years.
  • Full raw IoT, log, and event streams once aggregated metrics exist in warm/hot.
  • Long‑term snapshots, backups, and regulatory archives.
  • Large external datasets used mainly for ML experimentation.

Best practices:

  • Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store.
  • Design cold primarily for: Historical analytics, large‑slice ML training and forensic investigations.
  • Avoid designs where agents or business users need frequent single‑record lookups in cold.
  • Expose aggregated summaries (yearly/quarterly totals, high‑level KPIs) into warm/hot so everyday analytics don’t touch cold.
  • Implement automated lifecycle: Hot → Warm → Cold rules based on age and usage (e.g., after 18 months move to warm, after 3–5+ years to cold).
  • Customer‑managed data products: Built by customers in SAP Datasphere using Object store mainly from SAP sources (S/4HANA, ECC), non‑SAP data, or other data products. Replicated in Object Store→Data Product→shared via SQL on Files/Delta Sharing. Note: SAP-Managed Data Products are stored in the SAP BDC Foundation Services (FOS) layer, which is separate from the SAP Datasphere Object Store, although both use HDLF technology.

Cross‑tier recommendations (tying it all together):

  • Start from business SLAs and AI needs, not storage and Define clear policies:
    • Remote/Hot = real‑time and daily/weekly decisions (including agents).
    • Warm = regular analytics and planning.
    • Cold = retention, ML training, rare investigations.
  • Promote and demote based on real usage: Instrument and review query stats and Move only proven hotspots to hot, demote unused data to cold.
  • Use partitioning + lifecycle policies + automation instead of ad‑hoc moves to move data hot → warm → cold based on age and actual usage or vice-versa.

Conclusion:

Like we’ve seen throughout this article, data tiering is really about being intentional: put the right data in the right place for the right purpose. Hot and remote tiers serve real‑time decisions and AI agents, warm tiers power day‑to‑day reporting and planning, and cold tiers keep history available for audit and ML without wasting budget. SAP Datasphere and Business Data Cloud give you the building blocks, local in‑memory/disk tables, object storage, and remote access, to implement these tiers consistently. Once you design clear policies and align them with your business data, tables, data products and AI use cases, data tiering stops being a technical afterthought and becomes a core part of your business, analytics, planning and AI strategy.

References:

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE
  2. https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database

entry.title: Building a Sales Order Backlog Report Using Custom CDS Views (Sales + Billing)
entry.content:
Amartya Ghosh Dastidar, Senior SAP Developer


Introduction

Core Data Services (CDS) views are central to data modelling in SAP S/4HANA. A frequent requirement in the Sales and Distribution (SD) area is a sales order backlog report — the value and quantity of customer orders that have been placed but not yet invoiced. This post explains how to build such a report using custom CDS views that combine the Sales and Billing domains, covering the key techniques involved: joins, aggregation, calculated fields, type casting, null handling, and the correct treatment of cancelled and pro-forma invoices.

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA system with ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse
  • Basic knowledge of ABAP CDS and SQL
  • Familiarity with SD documents (sales orders, billing documents) and the display transactions VA03 / VF03

Understanding the Sales Order Backlog

In SD, a customer order flows through a chain of documents before revenue is recognised:

Sales Order  --->  Outbound Delivery  --->  Billing Document

            (delivery backlog)        (billing backlog)

The term “backlog” can refer to either of two gaps in this chain:

  1. Delivery backlog — orders placed but not yet delivered.
  2. Billing backlog — orders placed but not yet invoiced.

The report described here implements the billing view, defined as Backlog = Ordered Value − Billed Value. This definition determines the data model, because it requires the Sales domain and the Billing domain to be combined rather than reading open order quantities alone.

Standard CDS Views Used

The report is built on top of two released standard CDS views:

  1. I_SalesOrderItem — sales order line items (ordered quantity and net value).
  2. I_BillingDocumentItem — billing document line items (invoiced quantity and net value), which reference the originating sales order line via SalesDocument and SalesDocumentItem.

Solution Architecture

The solution uses a two-view (layered) design:

  1. ZIC_LIVEBILLING (helper view) — returns only valid, non-cancelled invoices per sales order item.
  2. ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG (main view) — joins the sales order item to the helper, aggregates billing, and calculates the backlog.

The layering is necessary because the cancellation logic requires a self-join that cannot be expressed inline within an aggregating view on all CDS compiler releases. Isolating it in a helper view also keeps the cleaning logic in one place and results in a cleaner, more maintainable model.

Step 1: Create the Helper View (ZIC_LIVEBILLING)

This view filters the billing document items down to genuine, live invoices — excluding pro-forma documents, cancellation documents, and cancelled originals.

Explanation

  1. SDDocumentCategory = 'M' — keeps only invoice-type documents; excludes pro-forma invoices.
  2. CancelledBillingDocument = '' — excludes the cancellation documents themselves.
  3. The self-join on C.CancelledBillingDocument = B.BillingDocument links each invoice to any document that cancelled it. The condition C.BillingDocument is null then keeps only invoices that were never cancelled, removing the cancelled originals.

Step 2: Create the Main View (ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG)

The main view joins the sales order item to the cleaned billing data, aggregates the billed values, and derives the backlog.

Key Techniques Explained

  1. Aggregation with GROUP BY — a single order line can be billed across several invoices (partial billing). Summing the billing per order key collapses these into one row and prevents the ordered value from being multiplied.
  2. Type casting — currency amounts of different internal lengths cannot be subtracted directly. Casting all amounts to abap.curr(23,2) (and quantities to abap.quan(13,3)) ensures type compatibility.
  3. COALESCE for null handling — an order line with no invoice returns null from the join; coalesce(..., 0) converts null to zero so the arithmetic remains valid.
  4. Calculated fields — the backlog quantity and value are derived inline as ordered minus billed.
  5. Session date — dats_days_between(RequestedDeliveryDate, $session.system_date) produces the number of days a line is overdue, enabling aging analysis.

Business Logic: Counting Only Valid Billing

Accurate backlog depends on summing only genuine, live invoices. Three categories must be excluded:

  1. Pro-forma invoices — not real sales; excluded via SDDocumentCategory = 'M'.
  2. Cancelled invoices — a cancelled invoice and its cancellation document both remain in the table; the self-join in the helper view removes both, leaving only the live invoice.
  3. Rejected order items — lines with a rejection reason are excluded via SalesDocumentRjcnReason = ''.

Note: the field that stores the cancellation reference can vary by release and may sit on the cancellation document rather than the original. The self-join approach is robust to this, because it identifies cancelled documents from the relationship rather than from a single status field.

Validation

The view should be validated against the source documents (VA03, VF03, and document flow) across the main data patterns:

  • Never billed — no invoice in the flow; backlog equals the full order value.
  • Partially billed — invoice covers part of the order; backlog equals the remaining value and quantity.
  • Fully billed — invoice equals the order; backlog is zero.
  • Cancelled invoice — only the live invoice is counted; backlog reflects the remaining value.

Where source data itself is inconsistent (for example a negatively priced item or a mis-keyed invoice amount), the view reflects the source faithfully. A reporting view should not silently correct source data; such cases are data-quality issues to be flagged separately.

Standard vs Custom CDS Views

Standard delivery/open-order backlog content is appropriate where a vanilla delivery backlog with standard fields is required. A custom view is justified when the requirement differs, as in this case:

  • The metric is ordered − billed (a billing backlog), which standard content does not frame the same way.
  • It combines Sales and Billing into one pre-shaped row with the specific fields and aging logic required for reporting.
  • It encodes system-specific billing rules (pro-forma and cancellation handling) validated against actual documents.

The trade-off is ongoing maintenance. Where standard content satisfies the same definition, it should be preferred to reduce maintenance overhead.

Key Points to Remember

  • Clarify the backlog definition (delivery vs billing) before designing the view.
  • The SQL view name is limited to 16 characters.
  • Aggregate one-to-many relationships before joining to avoid duplication.
  • Cast all amounts and quantities to a common type before arithmetic.
  • Exclude pro-forma, cancelled, and rejected documents from billed totals.
  • Use a layered design when complex filtering cannot be expressed inline.
  • Validate the output against source documents before release.

entry.title: From “AI in Applications” to “AI on Applications”
entry.content:
If you work hands‑on with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), you’ve likely noticed that AI strategy announcements and new platforms often introduce ambitious ideas and evolving narratives. It’s common to look for clear connections to how these changes influence modeling, governance, integration, and consumption of data in practice. With SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise strategy and the announcement of the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) at Sapphire 2026, many data professionals are asking whether this represents a real architectural shift or simply a reframing of existing capabilities.

This post is written for practitioners already familiar with SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud, and SAP Master Data Governance, and focuses on what this strategy means in technical and operational terms for data professionals working with BDC today. It also offers a completely new perspective to data professionals who have never worked with these technologies and are intrigued by SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise vision.

What is not changing

Before discussing what’s new, it’s important to establish what remains true.

The Autonomous Enterprise doesn’t imply unattended automation or a loss of human supervision. AI agents operate within boundaries defined by data models, semantics, authorizations, and governance policies. Human intent, stewardship, and architectural decisions remain foundational. BDC is also not being replaced. BAIP doesn’t sit alongside BDC as a competing data platform. It builds on BDC and depends on it. Without harmonized data, preserved business context, and governed semantics, the Autonomous Enterprise cannot function reliably.

If anything, this strategy reinforces that strong data foundations are not optional for AI, they are prerequisites.

Why SAP is changing the AI engagement model

Most enterprise AI to date has been embedded inside applications. Predictive scores, recommendations, or copilots are scoped to a single process or system, often with limited visibility across business domains. The Autonomous Enterprise introduces a different model: AI on applications. Instead of embedding AI inside each application, SAP is introducing an agent‑based engagement layer that operates across applications, workflows, and data domains. These agents can reason over business intent, traverse processes, and execute actions using governed enterprise data. This shift only works if AI can reliably understand business meaning at scale. That requirement is what makes BAIP and BDC inseparable.

BAIP from a data architecture perspective

From a technical standpoint, SAP Business AI Platform is not a single service or runtime. It is a composition of cloud services that bring together AI models, agents, orchestration, governance, and data access into a coherent platform. Within BAIP, BDC makes the core of “Contextualize and Reason” services along with SAP Knowledge Graph. Its role is to provide AI agents with access to data that already carries business semantics, lineage, and policy enforcement. This includes SAP‑managed data products, customer‑defined derived data products, and harmonized data across SAP and non‑SAP sources using zero‑copy and federated data access patterns.

With a deep business process expertise that no one can match, SAP systems contain the most critical business data, forming the center of gravity for data and AI applications. Just to illustrate the size of SAP’s metadata model, SAP Knowledge Graph for SAP S/4HANA alone is based on 452,000 ABAP tables, 80,000 CDS views, and 7.3 million fields. That said, AI agents do not reason over raw tables or loosely defined datasets. They reason over data products with defined meaning, relationships, and access rules. BDC is the system that supplies those constructs.

 SAP Business AI Platform

How this addresses real data practitioner challenges

For data practitioners, the relevance of the Autonomous Enterprise strategy becomes clear when mapped to long‑standing pain points. 

Data context loss across systems remains one of the most expensive problems in enterprise data landscapes. Even modern architectures often strip business meaning as data moves between systems. In the Autonomous Enterprise model, AI agents rely on BDC’s business data fabric to preserve object relationships, hierarchies, and semantics across SAP and non‑SAP environments.

Semantic inconsistency is another persistent issue. KPIs and dimensions drift as data is replicated and remodeled. Under BAIP, semantic definitions authored in BDC – through Datasphere models, BW objects, HANA calculation views, and governed master data – are reused not only for analytics, but also for AI‑driven reasoning and action.

Manual orchestration and governance overhead is the third major friction point. In the Autonomous Enterprise architecture, governance is enforced by design. AI agents discover and consume data through BDC services that already apply authorization policies, data product contracts, and stewardship rules. Governance becomes intrinsic to execution rather than an after‑the‑fact control.

How this maps to BDC components

At a practical level, the Autonomous Enterprise strategy doesn’t introduce a new data stack. It activates existing BDC components in a broader execution context.

  • SAP Datasphere continues to provide semantic modeling, data product definition, and cross‑system harmonization. Those models are no longer consumed only by dashboards and planners, but also by AI agents operating through BAIP.
  • SAP BW remains a governed source of enterprise semantics and historical business logic, with its models surfaced as data products that retain their meaning when consumed by analytics, planning, or AI.
  • SAP HANA Cloud contributes intelligent compute, advanced SQL, and increasingly AI‑aware capabilities that support both transactional and analytical data products used by agents and applications.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the primary analytical and planning consumption layer, while also becoming a beneficiary of AI‑assisted insight generation grounded in the same governed data products.
  • SAP Master Data Governance provides the quality and consistency layer that ensures AI agents reason over accurate, harmonized master data rather than fragmented representations.

Together, these components form the trusted knowledge core that BAIP relies on to make agent‑based AI reliable and explainable.

The technical takeaway

The Autonomous Enterprise strategy doesn’t reduce the importance of data engineering, modeling, or governance. It raises the bar. Putting AI on applications only works when AI can rely on consistent semantics, preserved business context, and enforced governance. BDC provides that foundation. BAIP operationalizes it. For data professionals, this strategy is less about adopting a new platform and more about extending existing BDC practices into an AI‑first execution model.

Join the conversation

These changes will surface new architectural patterns, design trade‑offs, and best practices. The Autonomous Enterprise will not be built in a single release. It will be built incrementally on the data foundations practitioners are already creating today. Join the Data Professionals community discussions to exchange technical perspectives, share lessons learned, and explore how others are evolving their BDC foundations to support agent‑based AI.

entry.title: Automate Recurring Data Updates in SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In a recent demo scenario, there was a need to automatically update product inventory records in SAP HANA Cloud when they had not been refreshed for more than 30 days. This is a common pattern for housekeeping and status management, and it can be handled neatly using the built-in scheduler in SAP HANA Cloud through SAP HANA Database Explorer.

For the scheduler syntax and options, refer to SAP Help: Create Scheduler Job Statement.

Assumed PRODUCT_INVENTORY Table Schema

For this example, assume there is a PRODUCT_INVENTORY table with the following structure:

CREATE COLUMN TABLE PRODUCT_INVENTORY (
    PRODUCT_ID NVARCHAR(20) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    PRODUCT_NAME NVARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    QUANTITY INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    INVENTORY_STATUS NVARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'ACTIVE', -- 'ACTIVE', 'OUTDATED'
    UPDATED_AT TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

The requirement is to mark records as OUTDATED when their UPDATED_AT value is older than 30 days and the current status is ACTIVE.

Use-case

Assume there is a PRODUCT_INVENTORY table and the requirement is to mark records as OUTDATED when their UPDATED_AT value is older than 30 days. This approach keeps the logic inside the database and works well for simple recurring operational tasks.

Step 1: Create the procedure

CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS ()
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY INVOKER
AS
BEGIN
    UPDATE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PRODUCT_INVENTORY
       SET INVENTORY_STATUS = 'OUTDATED',
           UPDATED_AT = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
     WHERE INVENTORY_STATUS = 'ACTIVE'
       AND UPDATED_AT < ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, -30);
END;

Step 2: Schedule the procedure

In SAP HANA Cloud, job scheduling is done with CREATE SCHEDULER JOB. The HANA Cloud cron format uses a 7-field pattern:

Year, Month, Day, DayOfWeek, Hour, Minute, Second

To run the procedure every day at midnight, use:

CREATE SCHEDULER JOB 
<YOUR_SCHEMA>.JOB_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY
CRON '* * * * 0 0 0'
ENABLE
PROCEDURE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS;

Run the procedure on demand

If you want to execute the procedure manually instead of waiting for the scheduled job, you can run it directly in SAP HANA Database Explorer using the CALL statement. This is useful for testing the procedure logic before enabling the scheduler or for triggering an immediate update when needed.

CALL <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS();

Monitor the job

After creating the job, you can verify it using the scheduler monitoring views in HANA Cloud. The scheduler metadata is available in M_SCHEDULER_JOBS, and the correct job-name column is SCHEDULER_JOB_NAME.

SELECT *
FROM M_SCHEDULER_JOBS
WHERE SCHEDULER_JOB_NAME = 'JOB_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY';

Clean-up

If the procedure and scheduler job are no longer needed, they can be removed with standard SQL statements in SAP HANA Cloud. Use DROP SCHEDULER JOB to delete the scheduled job and DROP PROCEDURE to remove the stored procedure.

DROP SCHEDULER JOB <YOUR_SCHEMA>.JOB_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY;

DROP PROCEDURE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS;

If needed, you can verify the objects are gone by checking M_SCHEDULER_JOBS and the procedure catalog views afterward.

Alternative scheduling options

For scenarios where scheduling should be managed outside the database, these are common alternatives on SAP BTP:

  • SAP Integration Suite - Timer Event
    Useful when the database update is one step in a broader integration or orchestration flow.
  • SAP Job Scheduling Service
    Suitable for centralized background job scheduling across BTP applications and services.
  • SAP Automation Pilot Service
    Commands for infrastructure operations can be scheduled within the SAP Automation Pilot, making it a good fit for maintenance and capacity management scenarios, for example: starting and stopping an SAP HANA Cloud instance at scheduled times.

Key Takeaways

For lightweight recurring database updates, the built-in SAP HANA Cloud scheduler is the simplest option because the logic stays inside HANA Cloud and is easy to maintain and monitor using SAP HANA Database Explorer. If the process spans multiple systems or requires platform-level orchestration, consider using SAP Integration Suite Timer Event, SAP Job Scheduling Service, or a custom CAP-based approach. For enterprise applications, CAP is often the recommended approach because it allows you to trigger CAP REST APIs on specific schedules or cron expressions without tying up your app’s core resources.

entry.title: Working with HANA Cloud Data base
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP HANA Cloud is a modern cloud database that helps organizations store, manage, and analyze data in real time. It provides powerful in-memory processing capabilities that enable faster application performance and better business decision-making.

In this blog, we will walk through the basics of working with SAP HANA Cloud, including database connectivity, table creation, data management, and SQL operations with practical examples.

 

Body:

 
Go to BTP Cockpit

 

 
Create HANA cloud instance
 

 

 
Add password and Whitelist IP so that the DB can be accessed from any IP address
 

 

 
 
 
 

 

 
Create tool instance to manage Hana DB
 

 

 
Provide roles to the user
 

 

 

 

 
 
Look into HANA tool application and wait for Hana DB instance to Runn
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Log into SQL console in HANA tool
 

 

 
 
Create Table 
 

 

 
 
Insert data
 
 

 

Summary: 

Summary:
SAP HANA Cloud makes it easy to store, manage, and analyze data in the cloud with high performance and scalability. By using its powerful database features, businesses can access insights faster and build efficient data-driven applications.

entry.title: Beyond Basic AI Error Handling in SAP CPI - A Rules-First Architecture with HANA Cloud Learning
entry.content:
The Problem Every Integration Developer Knows

Every SAP CPI developer has lived this scenario.

An iFlow fails in production at an inconvenient hour. You open the MPL log, stare at a cryptic stack trace, and spend the next 30–45 minutes manually cross-referencing adapter configurations, checking credentials, and testing payloads. By the time you identify the root cause  a null field in a mapping, an expired OAuth token, a malformed XML payload  the fix takes five minutes. The diagnosis took an hour.

Recent SAP Community posts have explored using the AI Adapter to automate this diagnosis, and the concept is genuinely sound. But the most common approach  sending the raw exception directly to an LLM has five problems that make it unsuitable for real production environments:

ProblemReal Impact
Raw payload sent to LLMBusiness data leaves the tenant — GDPR risk
Every error hits the LLMAuth failures and timeouts: unnecessary API cost at scale
No deduplicationSame error 1000 times = 1000 API calls
Free-text responseCannot be routed, filtered, or acted on programmatically
Response goes back to senderDevelopers never see the diagnosis

This post describes a different architecture — one that classifies errors deterministically first, sanitizes all business data before any AI call, caches intelligence in SAP HANA Cloud, and uses AI only for errors that are genuinely novel.

In my testing: 3 of 4 error types never touched the LLM at all.

What I Built: The ErrorAnalyzer Agent

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent is a native SAP CPI package  two iFlows, 13 Groovy scripts, a HANA Cloud free-tier instance, and the SAP AI Adapter configured with a breakthrough setting that is completely undocumented in SAP's official documentation.

Package contents:

  • EA_TestHarness — the main analyser iFlow, triggered via ProcessDirect
  • EA_VictimFlow — an example source iFlow that deliberately triggers errors to demonstrate the full pipeline

Technologies used:

  • SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)  iFlows, Groovy scripts, JDBC adapter, AI Adapter, Mail adapter
  • SAP HANA Cloud  free tier, with NLP vector engine enabled for V2
  • OpenRouter  LLM gateway (free tier models available)
  • OpenAI  via OpenRouter for AI diagnosis

Architecture Overview

The diagram below shows the complete three-zone architecture. Zone 1 handles detection inside SAP CPI. Zone 2 contains the intelligence layer — classification, sanitization, and dedup logic. Zone 3 is the AI and persistence layer.

 

 

The key design principle: rules first, AI only when necessary. Known errors (auth failures, timeouts, malformed payloads) are handled deterministically in milliseconds with zero LLM cost. Only genuinely novel or complex errors reach Claude.

Prerequisites

Before building, ensure you have the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite trial or production tenant with Cloud Integration capability enabled
  • OpenRouter account  free tier available at openrouter.ai (no credit card required for free models)
  • SAP HANA Cloud free tier instance provisioned on BTP (covered in setup steps below)
  • Gmail or SMTP account for email notifications
  • Postman or any REST client for testing

Step 1 — Security Material Setup

Before configuring any adapter, create two security artifacts in CPI Monitor.

1a. OpenRouter API Key

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → Secure Parameter

Field Value

NameOpenRouter_API_Key
TypeSecure Parameter
ValueBearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxxxx    ← include "Bearer " prefix — this is critical

Click Deploy. Wait for status to show Deployed  before proceeding.

Important: Do not store only the API key. Store the complete value including the "Bearer " prefix with a space. The AI Adapter uses this as the entire Authorization header value. This is part of the undocumented configuration covered in detail in Step 4.

 

1b. HANA Cloud User Credentials

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → User Credentials

Field Value

NameHANA_CLOUD_DB_USER
TypeUser Credentials
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour HANA instance DBADMIN password

Step 2 — HANA Cloud Setup

2a. Provision the HANA Cloud Instance

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → SAP HANA Cloud → select hana-free plan. Open HANA Cloud Central → Create Instance → SAP HANA Database. Enable the Natural Language Processing (NLP) feature in Advanced Settings  this activates the vector engine for V2 semantic search.

Free tier note: The instance stops every evening automatically and is deleted after 30 days of inactivity. Restart it manually when needed from HANA Cloud Central.

2b. Configure JDBC Data Source in CPI

Navigate to: Monitor → Manage JDBC Material → JDBC Data Source tab → Add

Field Value

NameHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Database TypeSAP HANA Cloud
JDBC URLjdbc:sap://<your-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443?encrypt=true&validateCertificate=true
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour DBADMIN password
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

Click Deploy → Test Connection. You should see a green success confirmation before proceeding.

 

2c. Create the Database Tables

Open HANA Cloud Central → SQL Console → connect using DBADMIN credentials → run the following SQL:

CREATE TABLE ERROR_PATTERNS (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    EXCEPTION_CLASS  NVARCHAR(255),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    ENVIRONMENT      NVARCHAR(20),
    AI_SUMMARY       NVARCHAR(500),
    ROOT_CAUSE       NVARCHAR(1000),
    RESOLUTION_STEPS NCLOB,
    SEVERITY         NVARCHAR(10),
    AUTO_RECOVERABLE NVARCHAR(5),
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    LAST_SEEN        TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FIRST_SEEN       TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    MPL_ID           NVARCHAR(100),
    RESOLVED         NVARCHAR(5)     DEFAULT 'false'
);

CREATE TABLE ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    LAST_ANALYZED    TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    CACHED_ANALYSIS  NCLOB
);

Step 3 — Build the EA_TestHarness iFlow

Create a new Integration Flow in your package. The complete flow structure is shown below.

 

 

The flow has the following step sequence:

  1. ProcessDirect Sender — address: /erroranalyzer/triage
  2. Content Modifier (setParams) — sets iflowName, environment, llmModel as Exchange Properties
  3. Classifier — Groovy script, categorises the error
  4. Sanitizer — Groovy script, masks payload values and generates fingerprint
  5. Router 2 — routes Non-LLM vs LLM-eligible errors
  6. DedupCheck — Groovy script, builds SELECT SQL for HANA lookup
  7. JDBC_SELECT Request Reply — executes SELECT against ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  8. ProcessDedupResult — Groovy script, parses JDBC XML response
  9. Router 3 — routes cache hit vs cache miss
  10. BuildCachedResponse / BuildPrompt — builds response from cache or builds LLM request
  11. Request Reply → AI Adapter — calls Claude via OpenRouter
  12. ParseLLMResponse → BuildEnhancedResponse — structures the AI analysis
  13. BuildEmailBody → Mail Receiver — sends HTML alert email
  14. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  15. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

Content Modifier — setParams configuration

Add a Content Modifier as the first step after Start. In the Exchange Property tab, add:

Property Name Type Value

   
environmentConstantDEV
llmModelConstantopenai/gpt-oss-120b:free  ← externalized, change without redeployment

Step 4 — The Classifier Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Classifier. This is the core of the rules-first approach — pure pattern matching, zero API cost, millisecond response.

Category Trigger Action Hits LLM

CONNECTIVITYConnectException, timeout, connection refusedRETRY_BACKOFFNo
AUTH_FAILUREHTTP 401/403, unauthorized, forbiddenREFRESH_CREDENTIALSNo
PAYLOAD_MALFORMEDSAXParseException, malformed XMLDEAD_LETTERNo
MAPPING_FAILUREMappingException, cannot convert, XSLT errorANALYZEYes
UNKNOWNEverything elseANALYZEYes
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    String exClass = (json.exceptionClass ?: "").toString()
    String exMsg   = (json.exceptionMessage ?: "").toString()

    String category; String action; boolean needsLLM = false

    if (exClass.contains("ConnectException") ||
        exMsg =~ /(?i)(timed out|timeout|connection refused|no route to host)/) {
        category = "CONNECTIVITY"; action = "RETRY_BACKOFF"
    } else if (exMsg =~ /(?i)statusCode:\s*40[13]/ ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(unauthorized|forbidden|invalid credentials)/) {
        category = "AUTH_FAILURE"; action = "REFRESH_CREDENTIALS"
    } else if (exClass.contains("SAXParse") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(content is not allowed in prolog|malformed)/) {
        category = "PAYLOAD_MALFORMED"; action = "DEAD_LETTER"
    } else if (exClass.contains("Mapping") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(xslt|mapping|cannot convert)/) {
        category = "MAPPING_FAILURE"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    } else {
        category = "UNKNOWN"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    }

    message.setProperty("errorCategory",    category)
    message.setProperty("suggestedAction",  action)
    message.setProperty("needsLLM",         needsLLM.toString())
    message.setProperty("exceptionClass",   exClass)
    message.setProperty("exceptionMessage", exMsg)
    message.setProperty("rawPayload",       (json.payload ?: "").toString())
    if (json.mplId)       message.setProperty("mplId",       json.mplId.toString())
    if (json.iflowName)   message.setProperty("iflowName",   json.iflowName.toString())
    if (json.environment) message.setProperty("environment", json.environment.toString())
    return message
}

Step 5 — The Sanitizer Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Sanitizer. This script parses the raw payload — XML or JSON — and replaces every leaf value with a typed token before anything leaves the tenant.

A customer record like this:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "ORD-001",
    "amount": null,
    "customer": "John Smith",
    "taxId": "DE123456789"
  }
}

Becomes this — safe to send to any external LLM:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "[STRING:7]",
    "amount": "[EMPTY]",
    "customer": "[STRING:10]",
    "taxId": "[STRING:11]"
  }
}

The Sanitizer also generates a fingerprint: MD5(iFlowName + exceptionClass + errorCategory). Including iFlowName ensures that the same MappingException in two different iFlows produces different fingerprints — preventing wrong cross-iFlow cache hits.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlParser
import groovy.util.XmlNodePrinter
import groovy.util.Node
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import java.security.MessageDigest

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String raw = ((message.getProperty("rawPayload") ?: "").toString()).trim()
    String sanitized
    try {
        if (raw.startsWith("<")) {
            def root = new XmlParser().parseText(raw)
            maskXmlNode(root)
            def sw = new StringWriter()
            new XmlNodePrinter(new PrintWriter(sw)).print(root)
            sanitized = sw.toString().trim()
        } else if (raw.startsWith("{") || raw.startsWith("[")) {
            def obj = new JsonSlurper().parseText(raw)
            sanitized = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(maskJson(obj)))
        } else if (raw.isEmpty()) {
            sanitized = "[EMPTY]"
        } else {
            sanitized = maskValue(raw)
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        sanitized = "[UNPARSEABLE: ${e.message}]"
    }
    String fpSource = (message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "").toString()
    message.setProperty("sanitizedPayload", sanitized)
    message.setProperty("fingerprint", md5(fpSource))
    return message
}

void maskXmlNode(Node node) {
    if (node.children().any { it instanceof Node }) {
        node.children().each { if (it instanceof Node) maskXmlNode(it) }
    } else { node.setValue(maskValue(node.text())) }
}

def maskJson(obj) {
    if (obj instanceof Map) { def m = [:]; obj.each { k, v -> m[k] = maskJson(v) }; return m }
    else if (obj instanceof List) { return obj.collect { maskJson(it) } }
    else { return maskValue(obj == null ? "" : obj.toString()) }
}

String maskValue(String v) {
    if (v == null || v.trim().isEmpty()) return "[EMPTY]"
    v = v.trim()
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+/)                return "[INT:${v.length()}]"
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+\.\d+/)           return "[DECIMAL]"
    if (v ==~ /\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}.*/) return "[DATE]"
    if (v ==~ /(?i)(true|false)/)     return "[BOOL]"
    return "[STRING:${v.length()}]"
}

String md5(String s) {
    MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5").digest(s.bytes).encodeHex().toString()
}

Step 6 — Router Configuration

Add a Router step after the Sanitizer. Configure two routes:

Router 2 — routes based on whether LLM is needed:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (LLM path)${property.needsLLM} = 'true'Non-XML
Non_LLM (default)Default Route — checkedN/A

Router 3 — routes based on HANA cache result:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (cache hit)${property.cacheHit} = 'true'Non-XML
Default (cache miss)Default Route — checkedN/A

 

 

 

Step 7 — HANA Dedup Check via JDBC

On the LLM path, before calling Claude, the system checks HANA Cloud. Add these three steps in sequence:

  1. DedupCheck — Groovy script that builds the SELECT SQL
  2. Request Reply → JDBC Receiver — executes the query against HANA
  3. ProcessDedupResult — parses the XML response from JDBC

Important: CPI's JDBC adapter returns query results as XML — not JSON. The structure is ROOT → select_response → row. Use groovy.util.XmlSlurper (not groovy.xml.XmlSlurper — that class does not exist in CPI's Groovy runtime).

// ProcessDedupResult — parse JDBC XML response
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    try {
        def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(body)
        def rows = xml.select_response.row
        if (rows && rows.size() > 0) {
            def row = rows[0]
            String cachedAnalysis = row.CACHED_ANALYSIS.text()
            String occCount = row.OCCURRENCE_COUNT.text() ?: "1"
            if (cachedAnalysis) {
                message.setProperty("cacheHit", "true")
                message.setProperty("cachedAnalysis", cachedAnalysis)
                message.setProperty("cacheOccurrenceCount",
                    (occCount.toInteger() + 1).toString())
            } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
        } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    } catch(Exception e) { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    return message
}

JDBC Receiver Adapter configuration for both SELECT and WRITE steps:

Field Value

JDBC Data Source AliasHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

 

Step 8 — The Undocumented AI Adapter Configuration

This section is the most important technical finding in this post.

If you have tried connecting SAP's AI Adapter (Custom-OpenAI-REST) to OpenRouter or any external LLM endpoint, you have almost certainly encountered this:

401 Unauthorized — Missing Authentication Header

It does not matter how many times you redeploy the Secure Parameter. Basic mode simply does not work with OpenRouter. After extensive testing, I identified the exact combination of three settings that resolves this completely.

Fix 1 — Configuration Type: Advanced (not Basic)

In the Processing tab, change Configuration Type from Basic to Advanced. Basic mode uses SAP's internal OpenAI abstraction layer which is incompatible with OpenRouter's authentication format. Advanced mode gives direct REST control.

 

 

 

Fix 2 — Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter

Store the full Authorization value — not just the key — in the Secure Parameter: Bearer sk-or-v1-xxx. The adapter uses this as the complete Authorization header value. Without the prefix, the header is malformed and the request fails.

Fix 3 — Split the URL across tabs

Connection tab and Processing tab must share the URL:

  • Connection tab Address: https://openrouter.ai
  • Processing tab Relative URL: /api/v1/chat/completions

 

Complete Working Configuration

Connection Tab:

Field Value

Message ProtocolCustom-OpenAI-REST
Addresshttps://openrouter.ai
API Key AliasOpenRouter_API_Key
Connection Timeout60000 ms
Response Timeout60000 ms

 

Processing Tab:

Field Value

Configuration TypeAdvanced ← Critical — Basic fails with 401
HTTP MethodPOST
Relative URL/api/v1/chat/completions
Request Payload SourceExchange Body
Request HeadersHTTP-Referer|X-Title|Content-Type

 

Step 9 — Build the LLM Request (BuildPrompt)

The BuildPrompt Groovy script assembles the request body for Claude. It reads the sanitized context from message properties — never from the raw payload — and forces a structured JSON response format.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String errorCategory = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "UNKNOWN"
    String exClass       = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String exMsg         = message.getProperty("exceptionMessage") ?: ""
    String sanitized     = message.getProperty("sanitizedPayload") ?: "[EMPTY]"
    String iflowName     = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown"
    String environment   = message.getProperty("environment") ?: "Unknown"
    String llmModel      = message.getProperty("llmModel") ?: "anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku"

    String userContent = """\
Analyze this SAP CPI runtime error and respond with ONLY valid JSON.

Required structure:
{
  "error_summary": "one clear sentence",
  "root_cause": "specific technical cause",
  "resolution_steps": ["step 1", "step 2", "step 3"],
  "auto_recoverable": false,
  "severity": "HIGH"
}

Severity: HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW only.
auto_recoverable: boolean only.

Error details:
iFlow: ${iflowName}
Environment: ${environment}
Category: ${errorCategory}
Exception class: ${exClass}
Exception message: ${exMsg}
Sanitized payload structure (real values masked):
${sanitized}"""

    def requestBody = [
        model: llmModel,
        messages: [
            [role: "system", content: "You are an SAP CPI integration expert. Respond only with valid JSON."],
            [role: "user",   content: userContent]
        ],
        temperature: 0.2,
        max_tokens: 400
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(requestBody))
    return message
}

Step 10 — EA_VictimFlow: Wiring the Exception Subprocess

EA_VictimFlow demonstrates how to connect any existing iFlow to the ErrorAnalyzer. The pattern is reusable — copy the exception subprocess to any failing iFlow by changing only the iflowName constant.

 

Add a Content Modifier at the start of EA_VictimFlow with these Exchange Properties:

Property Name Type Value

iflowNameConstantEA_VictimFlow
environmentConstantDEV

Inside the Exception Subprocess, add a Groovy script named ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def exCaught   = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")
    String exClass = exCaught ? exCaught.getClass().getName() : "Unknown"
    String exMsg   = exCaught ? exCaught.getMessage() : "Unknown error"
    String mplId   = message.getHeader("SAP_MessageProcessingLogID", String.class) ?: "unavailable"
    String iflowName = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown_iFlow"
    String payload = ""
    try { payload = message.getBody(String) ?: "" } catch(Exception e) { payload = "" }

    def envelope = [
        exceptionClass:   exClass,
        exceptionMessage: exMsg,
        payload:          payload,
        mplId:            mplId,
        iflowName:        iflowName,
        environment:      message.getProperty("environment") ?: "DEV"
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(envelope))
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")
    return message
}

Connect the ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder to a ProcessDirect Receiver with address: /erroranalyzer/triage

Important: SAP_ApplicationID is NOT the iFlow name — it is the application binding ID. Always use a Content Modifier constant for iflowName as shown above.

Step 10b — Writing to HANA Cloud

After Claude returns the analysis, two separate JDBC write steps persist the intelligence to HANA. They are intentionally split into two steps because CPI's JDBC adapter executes exactly one SQL statement per call — chaining two statements with a semicolon throws a syntax error.

PrepareHANAWrite — writes full analysis to ERROR_PATTERNS

This script builds a MERGE statement that either inserts a new error record or increments the occurrence count if the fingerprint already exists. Note the HANA-specific syntax: WITH PRIMARY KEY is required — standard WHERE syntax fails with a syntax error.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llm = new JsonSlurper().parseText(
                  message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}")

    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")    ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory")  ?: ""
    String exClass     = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")      ?: ""
    String environment = message.getProperty("environment")    ?: ""
    String mplId       = message.getProperty("mplId")          ?: ""
    String aiSummary   = (llm.error_summary   ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String rootCause   = (llm.root_cause       ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String steps       = JsonOutput.toJson(llm.resolution_steps ?: []).replace("'","''")
    String severity    = (llm.severity         ?: "MEDIUM").toString()
    String autoRec     = (llm.auto_recoverable ?: false).toString()
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_PATTERNS
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, EXCEPTION_CLASS, IFLOW_NAME,
         ENVIRONMENT, AI_SUMMARY, ROOT_CAUSE, RESOLUTION_STEPS,
         SEVERITY, AUTO_RECOVERABLE, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
         LAST_SEEN, FIRST_SEEN, MPL_ID, RESOLVED)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${exClass}',
                '${iflowName}','${environment}','${aiSummary}',
                '${rootCause}','${steps}','${severity}',
                '${autoRec}', 1,'${now}','${now}','${mplId}','false')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

PrepareDedup — writes fast lookup entry to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llmRaw         = message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}"
    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")   ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")     ?: ""
    String cached      = llmRaw.replace("'","''")
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, IFLOW_NAME,
         LAST_ANALYZED, OCCURRENCE_COUNT, CACHED_ANALYSIS)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${iflowName}',
                '${now}', 1,'${cached}')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

The complete write sequence after Claude analysis:

  1. BuildEnhancedResponse — assembles the final JSON response
  2. BuildEmailBody — builds the HTML alert email
  3. Mail Receiver — dispatches the email notification
  4. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  5. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  6. End

Both JDBC_WRITE and JDBC_WRITE2 use the same receiver adapter configuration as JDBC_SELECT  Data Source Alias: HANA_Cloud_JDBC, timeouts: 30 seconds.

Email is dispatched before the HANA writes so that if HANA is unavailable (free tier stops nightly), the developer notification still goes out regardless.

Step 11 — Testing

Deploy both iFlows. Test using any REST client by POSTing to the EA_VictimFlow HTTPS endpoint with plain text body:

Request Body Error Category Expected Behaviour

mappingMAPPING_FAILUREHits Claude, writes to HANA, sends email with AI analysis
authAUTH_FAILURENo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
timeoutCONNECTIVITYNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
xmlPAYLOAD_MALFORMEDNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email

Results

First Run — MAPPING_FAILURE (new error, hits Claude)

{
  "fingerprint":     "dfb60537dd72852be164ee7f46bab45a",
  "mplId":           "AGoi2tHxrNFx-9CloVUzH46UcB-6",
  "iflowName":       "EA_VictimFlow",
  "environment":     "DEV",
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "suggestedAction": "ANALYZE",
  "sanitizedPayload": "{\"order\":{\"id\":\"[STRING:7]\",\"amount\":\"[EMPTY]\"}}",
  "servedFromCache": "false",
  "llmParseSuccess": "true",
  "aiAnalysis": {
    "summary":   "Mapping failed because null Amount field cannot be converted to decimal",
    "rootCause": "Source system sent null where decimal value was expected",
    "resolutionSteps": [
      "Validate Amount field in source payload before mapping",
      "Add null check in Groovy mapping script before decimal conversion",
      "Define default value for optional numeric fields"
    ],
    "autoRecoverable": false,
    "severity": "HIGH"
  }
}

 

Second Run — Same Error, HANA Cache Hit

{
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "servedFromCache": "true",
  "occurrenceCount": "2",
  "aiAnalysis": { ...same analysis returned instantly, zero LLM call... }
}

 

The real MPL ID is captured in every response — developers can jump directly to the processing log without searching.

HANA Cloud as a Learning Store

Every new error diagnosis is permanently stored. After running for several weeks:

-- Top recurring failures
SELECT IFLOW_NAME, ERROR_CATEGORY, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
       AI_SUMMARY, SEVERITY, FIRST_SEEN, LAST_SEEN
FROM ERROR_PATTERNS
ORDER BY OCCURRENCE_COUNT DESC;

-- All unresolved HIGH severity errors
SELECT * FROM ERROR_PATTERNS
WHERE SEVERITY = 'HIGH' AND RESOLVED = 'false'
ORDER BY LAST_SEEN DESC;

 

This gives you a persistent error intelligence dashboard that no standard CPI monitoring tool provides natively. The more errors the system sees, the richer this store becomes.

Email Notifications

Every error  known or novel  triggers an email alert to the development team. Known errors (auth, timeout, malformed) receive a lightweight notification with category and suggested action. Novel errors with Claude analysis receive the full HTML email with root cause, resolution steps, and MPL ID link.

 

 

Honest Limitations

This is a V1 implementation on a trial tenant. To be transparent:

  • Synchronous design: The LLM call adds 3–8 seconds to the exception path. Production deployment should decouple via JMS queue — not available on trial tenants.
  • Exception message not sanitized: The payload is fully masked but exception message text is sent as-is. Real SAP exceptions can embed field values. Exception message sanitization is planned for V2.
  • No auto-remediation: suggestedAction is informational only. Phase B adds deterministic auto-recovery actions (retry, credential refresh) for known error types.
  • Free model availability: OpenRouter free models go offline without notice. For production, use SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub or a paid OpenRouter tier.

What Is Next: Joule Studio Integration via MCP

Phase 2 exposes the ErrorAnalyzer as an MCP tool and connects it to SAP Joule Studio via the MCP Gateway announced at SAP Sapphire 2026. The goal: ask Joule in natural language — "What failed in my OrderProcessing flow in the last hour?" — and have it call the ErrorAnalyzer tool, query the HANA learning store, and return a conversational diagnosis grounded in your tenant's actual error history.

Phase 3 replaces the exact-match fingerprint with semantic vector similarity using HANA Cloud's NLP engine , already enabled on the instance. Similar errors that differ only in variable values will match correctly, dramatically improving cache hit rates and diagnosis quality.

Summary

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent demonstrates that AI error handling in SAP CPI does not have to be expensive, risky, or shallow.

  • A rules-first classifier keeps 75% of errors away from the LLM entirely — zero cost, millisecond response
  • A payload sanitizer ensures no business data leaves the tenant — the LLM sees shapes, not values
  • A HANA dedup cache means the same error is diagnosed once — every subsequent occurrence is instant and free
  • A HANA learning store accumulates error intelligence permanently — the system gets smarter and cheaper the longer it runs
  • The AI Adapter breakthrough — Advanced mode, Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter, split URL — is the practical finding I hope saves other developers the hours of debugging it cost to discover

If you build on this or encounter any issues replicating the AI Adapter configuration, please comment below. The complete Groovy scripts are available on request.

entry.title: The AI Isn't the Problem. The Data Underneath It Is.
entry.content:
Most AI projects stall in the same place. The pilot worked. The model performed. Leadership approved the next phase. And then, without a clean explanation, the path to production got complicated.

It's rarely the AI. It's almost always the data.

Two-thirds of business leaders say they don't fully trust their data. For organizations trying to move AI from experiment to enterprise capability, that's not a survey result - it's a structural problem. The gap between a convincing proof of concept and a scalable, reliable AI deployment is, in most cases, a data readiness gap.

This month, that gap gets a framework.

---

## What's coming

The Open Data Institute (ODI), SAP's independent research partner on the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI (IDEA), is releasing A Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data in late June. It's a rigorous, vendor-agnostic treatment of the problem: built from a comparative analysis of 23 published frameworks from organizations including Gartner, McKinsey, EY, Accenture, and Google Cloud, validated through expert interviews with senior data leaders at NTT Data, Clorox, and ZEISS, and grounded in 14 years of ODI experience building independent, trustworthy data infrastructure.

The result is 21 concrete, actionable criteria across four pillars - dataset properties, metadata, surrounding infrastructure, and governance - each calibrated for the enterprise environment and the specific demands of agentic AI.

It's not a whitepaper with broad recommendations. It's a practical checklist.

---

## The shape of the problem

The framework defines AI-ready data as "data that is technically optimized for machine learning, high quality and adherent to standards, legally compliant, and responsibly collected."

What makes the enterprise version significantly harder is scale, interconnection, and the nature of how AI now interacts with data.

Enterprise data doesn't live in tidy, isolated repositories. A customer record exists in the CRM, billing, and logistics systems - each version slightly different, each updated independently. A product code means one thing in finance and something adjacent in supply chain. That fragmentation is manageable when humans navigate it; people apply judgment, know which system to trust for which question. AI agents don't do that. They use what they can access, and they use it with confidence.

The problem compounds with agentic AI - systems that don't just answer queries but independently search for data, write and run code to analyze it, take actions based on what they find, and generate new data as a byproduct. That interaction pattern introduces failure modes that traditional data pipelines don't surface. A schema changes silently. A data feed shifted.
